topic: Los/las, por vs para, and their idioms

tips.title: Los and las are just el and la, pluralized
tips.body: You already know el and la; los and las simply add 'more than one' to the mix. Los covers masculine plural nouns, las covers feminine plural nouns — the same gender-matching habit you've been building all week, now with an -s.

tips.title: Por looks backward, para looks forward
tips.body: Por explains the cause, reason, or means behind something — it looks back in time. Para points at a goal, destination, or recipient — it looks ahead. 'I did it por love' (the reason) versus 'I did it para you' (the recipient) captures the split.

tips.title: Three idioms hide inside por and para
tips.body: Por qué (why?), por eso (that's why), and para que (so that/in order that) are fixed phrases built from words you already know. Notice that por qué carries an accent mark because it's asking a question — that accent is the giveaway.

tips.title: En for being in a spot, con for being with someone
tips.body: En usually marks location or being inside/on something ('en the car'), while con marks accompaniment or a tool ('con a friend', 'con a pen'). They're small words, but you'll use them constantly.

bonusCards.english: The children are in the park with the dogs.
Why are the doors closed? Because of the storm.
bonusCards.answer: **¿Por qué** are **las** doors closed? **Por** the storm.

bonusCards.english: I bought the gifts for the girls.
That's why I study with a book and a pen.
I called so that the doctors know the truth.
bonusCards.answer: I called **para que** **los** doctors know **la** truth.
